title: feat: chalk archive — compact released tasks + old events into .chalk/archive

acceptanceCriteria.text: chalk archive moves done+released tasks (stamped archivedAt) to .chalk/archive/tasks-<year>.json and only their event lines to updates-<year>.jsonl; global events and other tasks' events stay live
a candidate referenced by a remaining task's after is KEPT with a printed reason; done-unreleased tasks stay (release idempotency); archived tasks are never re-released
true
the portal still shows archived delivered scope (portalModel reads the archive); backlog stays coherent after compaction

findings.note: Criterion 4 'backlog stays coherent after compaction' is only asserted by `chalk backlog` exiting 0. The dangling-after-edge risk is avoided by design (dep-referenced candidates are KEPT, and B is kept in the fixture), so no archived task can be a live after-target — but there is no direct negative test that a dependency edge into the archive is impossible; coherence rests on the KEEP rule rather than an explicit assertion.

The printed KEEP 'reason' text (`dep-referenced by ...`) is not asserted verbatim — only `/kept B ships/` is matched. The presence of a human reason string is thus not strictly pinned, though the criterion ('KEPT with a printed reason') is substantively covered.

The post-archive `emitUpdate` progress-update and the exact dry-run event count (countEvents) are untested cosmetics; harmless. archivedTasks correctly returns [] when the archive dir is absent, so portalModel's new merge introduces no regression for non-archived projects.

title: chore: cut v0.1.0 via chalk release, seed v0.2 roadmap issues, enable GitHub Discussions

acceptanceCriteria.text: chalk-protocol@0.1.0 is live on the npm registry and npx chalk-protocol demo completes green from the registry on a clean directory
v0.2 roadmap issues seeded and labeled (#78-#86 + #88 evidence-push + #89 retro-filed); GitHub Discussions enabled
v0.1.0 git tag pushed with a GitHub release; post-release chalk archive compacted the spine (48 tasks, 190KB→4.3KB tasks.json)

findings.note: Criterion 1 (chalk-protocol@0.1.0 live on npm + `npx chalk-protocol demo` green from registry on a clean dir) is entirely external and has zero in-repo evidence. The diff only flips the task from blocked→in-progress and logs a self-authored 'Released v0.1.0' decision in updates.jsonl — a claim, not proof. No registry check, no tarball, no publish workflow run output is present.

Criterion 2 (v0.2 roadmap issues #78-#86, #88, #89 seeded/labeled; GitHub Discussions enabled) is wholly unverifiable from this change. Nothing in the diff or working tree references these issues, labels, or Discussions state. The change provides no artifact demonstrating the issues exist or Discussions were turned on.

The task ships real behavior (a release + external roadmap/repo config) with NO locked tests and NO asserting artifact. Every criterion could be false while this diff still applies cleanly — it is a vacuous, self-declared completion. Per the hard rule, behavior added with no test asserting it must block.

Criterion 3 is only partially evidenced: the archive did compact the spine (48 tasks moved to .chalk/archive/tasks-2026.json, 3 tasks remain live), which is checkable. But the git tag v0.1.0 push and the GitHub release cannot be confirmed from the diff — `git tag`/release state is not shown, and the exact '190KB→4.3KB' figure is unverified.

The diff records the task as merely 'in-progress' with pending todos, inconsistent with a change presented as having completed the v0.1.0 cut; the metadata state does not itself substantiate that the release chore is finished.

title: fix: chalk release --commit — commit CHANGELOG+version bump, then tag that commit (removes the release.yml tag-normalization step)

acceptanceCriteria.text: chalk release --commit writes CHANGELOG.md + the package.json bump, creates a git commit containing exactly those release artifacts (conventional message chore(release): vX.Y.Z), then tags vX.Y.Z on that commit — git show vX.Y.Z:package.json reports the bumped version
tag-collision safety is preserved under --commit: when tag vX.Y.Z already exists the command dies BEFORE writing CHANGELOG, bumping package.json, committing, or marking any task released
without --commit the existing tag-first behavior is unchanged (all existing release tests stay green)
release.yml drops the 'npm pkg set version' tag-normalization step (the tagged tree is published as-is) and its header comment reflects the commit-then-tag flow
chalk help documents the --commit flag on the release line

findings.note: test/package.test.mjs was legitimately amended (amend-spec logged in .chalk/decisions.md) and its assertion now requires the ABSENCE of 'npm pkg set version', but the file's header comment (lines 6–7) still says the workflow 'normalizes the version from the tag (chalk tags before the bump lands)' — stale prose contradicting the flipped assertion; should have been cleaned in the same amendment.

Partial-failure edge under --commit: the collision is probed up front (gitTry rev-parse), but if `git tag` fails AFTER the release commit for a non-collision reason (hook, perms, or a tag created in the probe→tag window), the die() leaves an untagged chore(release) commit at HEAD with the bumped package.json; a re-run then computes the NEXT version from the already-bumped version (version skip). Criterion 2 (pre-existing tag dies before writing) is still satisfied and tested, but this recovery path is untested and slightly worse than tag-first.

test/release-commit.test.mjs — the locked contract for this task — does not appear in the git diff --stat (8 files, none of them this file), implying it is untracked-new in the working tree. It exists and its sha256 is pinned in .chalk/tasks.json, but make sure it is committed with the change; otherwise CI (node --test in release.yml) and any fresh checkout would run without the very tests that pin --commit behavior.

--commit --no-tag skips the up-front collision probe entirely (wantTag=false), committing the bump with no tag check — consistent with --no-tag semantics and the documented escape hatch, but the combination is untested; noting for completeness, not a blocker.

reviews.note: locked test amended after review — re-review required

findings.note: test/release-commit.test.mjs — the locked contract pinning every --commit behavior — does not appear in git diff --stat (8 changed files, none of them this one), meaning it is an untracked NEW file in the working tree. Its sha256 is pinned in .chalk/tasks.json and it exists on disk, but if it is not committed with the change, CI (node --test in release.yml) and any fresh checkout run WITHOUT the tests that guard criteria 1–3 and 5 — a vacuous green. Ensure `git add test/release-commit.test.mjs` lands in the same commit before `chalk done`.

Partial-failure path under --commit: the collision is probed up front (gitTry rev-parse refs/tags/vX.Y.Z), but if `git tag` fails AFTER the release commit for a non-collision reason (hook, perms, probe→tag race), die() leaves an untagged chore(release) commit at HEAD with the bumped package.json; a re-run then bumps FROM the already-bumped version (version skip) and would produce a second release commit. Criterion 2 (pre-existing tag) is satisfied and tested; this adjacent recovery path is untested and slightly worse than tag-first.
--commit --no-tag skips the up-front collision probe entirely (wantTag=false), committing the bump with no tag check; the combination is consistent with --no-tag semantics but exercised by no test.

Criterion 4's second clause ('header comment reflects the commit-then-tag flow') is prose-only: the locked test asserts the absence of `npm pkg set version` but nothing about the comment. Verified manually that release.yml lines 5–7 now describe commit-then-tag; acceptable since comments are not machine-testable behavior.

test/package.test.mjs (locked) was modified, but via two sanctioned amend-spec entries logged in .chalk/decisions.md (flip the workflow assertion to forbid the normalization step; sweep the stale header prose the prior review flagged), the sha256 is re-pinned in tasks.json, and this re-review covers the post-amendment state — the legitimate path was followed.

title: fix: CONFIG.md drift gate only validates top-level protocol keys

acceptanceCriteria.text: Recurse into nested protocol.* keys (dotted paths) when comparing documented vs initialized config
Add a test asserting a documented-but-removed nested key (and an undocumented new nested key) fails the gate

issue.body:
test/docs.test.mjs compares docs/CONFIG.md against `Object.keys(meta.protocol)` — only top-level keys. Nested keys (verify.test, review.requiredAt, github.mergeMethod, handoff.maxAttempts, etc.) can drift from the docs or be added to initSpine without the gate noticing, so the intended 'every protocol.* key is documented' guarantee holds only at the top level.

- [ ] Recurse into nested protocol.* keys (dotted paths) when comparing documented vs initialized config
- [ ] Add a test asserting a documented-but-removed nested key (and an undocumented new nested key) fails the gate

_filed by `chalk retro` (self-healing)_

findings.note: configDrift checks exactly ONE nested level, not true recursion: level-3 keys (e.g. protocol.github.labelType.bug) are explicitly out of scope. Criterion 1 says 'recurse into nested protocol.* keys'. Mitigated: every schema-bearing key in initSpine is at depth ≤2 (deeper levels are data maps / gate value shapes), the scope choice is documented in the code comment, logged via the sanctioned amend-spec decision, and locked by a dedicated test ('recursion stops one level deep'), so the shipped behavior matches the locked contract.

False-negative edge: `if (!isPlainObj(val) || !Object.keys(val).length) continue;` means if a protocol key's default becomes an EMPTY object (all nested keys removed) or degrades to a scalar/array while the doc still lists a `{ … }` key list, the documented-but-removed nested keys are NOT flagged. Criterion 2's specific single-key-removal scenario is covered, but the all-keys-removed case silently passes and no test pins it.

The `{ … }` key list is taken from the FIRST inline-code brace group in a section (`/`\{([^}`]*)\}/`). If future prose adds an illustrative `{ … }` snippet before the real key list (as `verify` already does after its list), the gate compares against the wrong group. Duplicate `### `key`` headings also silently overwrite each other in the sections Map.

lib/store.mjs default-config changes (review.required→requiredAt:[], regression.locPerTest:2000, github.ciPollIntervalMs/ciPollAttempts) are scope creep for a docs-drift task, but they are forced by the new gate (docs already documented those keys), behavior-neutral (reviewCadences, heldOutFloor, and brokecheck all carry identical fallbacks), and covered — reverting store.mjs alone now fails the real-config drift tests.

Test adequacy is genuinely strong: test/config-drift.test.mjs asserts both required negative cases (undocumented NEW nested key, documented-but-REMOVED nested key) with exact problem counts and dotted-path messages, plus bidirectional top-level drift, missing-key-list failure, and a non-vacuity guard (verify must still carry ≥4 nested keys). Reverting to the old top-level-only gate would fail these tests, so the change is non-vacuously covered. Locked test/docs.test.mjs was modified, but via the sanctioned amend-spec path (decision logged, sha256 re-pinned in tasks.json).

reviews.note: locked test amended after review — re-review required

findings.note: Criterion 1 says 'recurse' but configDrift() checks exactly ONE nested level: depth-3 keys (e.g. protocol.github.labelType.bug) are explicitly out of scope. Mitigated: CONFIG.md's format can only express one level of schema (a `{ … }` key list per section), every schema-bearing initSpine key is at depth ≤2 (deeper levels are data maps / value shapes), the scope is documented in the code comment, logged via the sanctioned amend-spec decisions, and pinned by a dedicated locked test ('recursion stops one level deep'). The shipped behavior satisfies the issue's intent (nested keys can no longer drift silently) and both required negative cases emit dotted-path problems (protocol.verify.build, protocol.handoff.maxAttempts).

Criterion 2 is directly and non-vacuously covered: test/config-drift.test.mjs asserts the undocumented-NEW-nested-key and documented-but-REMOVED-nested-key failures with exact problem counts and dotted-path regexes, plus the degraded-to-scalar/empty-object edge (both '' and {}), bidirectional top-level drift, the missing-`{ … }`-list failure, the one-level scope, and a real-config non-vacuity guard (verify must carry ≥4 nested keys). Reverting configDrift or the store.mjs default additions alone fails these tests. Note: test/config-drift.test.mjs does not appear in the diff stat — it is presumably a new untracked file; confirm it is committed alongside the change, or the locked pin in tasks.json points at a file that never ships.

The `{ … }` key list is the FIRST `` `{ `` group in a section (/`\{([^}`]*)\}/). If future prose inserts an illustrative brace snippet before the real key list (the review section already carries `{"verdict":…}` later in its body), the gate compares the wrong group — this fails LOUD (false positive), not silent, so drift cannot slip through, but it is a maintenance trap. Duplicate `### `key`` headings also silently overwrite each other in the sections Map.

lib/store.mjs default changes (review.required:false → requiredAt:[], regression.locPerTest:2000, github.ciPollIntervalMs:5000/ciPollAttempts:24) are scope creep for a docs-drift task, but they are forced by the now-honest gate (docs documented those keys), behavior-neutral for new inits (reviewCadences honors legacy required, heldOutFloor defaults to 2000, brokecheck falls back to 5000/24), and cannot touch existing configs (initSpine only runs at init). The locked test/docs.test.mjs was modified, but via the sanctioned amend-spec path — two decisions logged, sha256 re-pinned, prior review correctly marked stale triggering this re-review.

title: fix: evidence-push failures are swallowed (catch{}) — blob-SHA 404s surface as broken PR images

acceptanceCriteria.text: a failed git push in chalk evidence is no longer swallowed: a visible ⚠ warning line names the push failure and its cause
when the push fails, the PR body is NOT edited with blob-SHA image URLs (no 404 images land in the PR); the final status line says the screenshots were captured but not attached
when the push succeeds, behavior is unchanged: the evidence markdown with commit-SHA blob URLs is appended to the PR body

an e2e test drives the REAL push path via the bare-remote + stub-gh fixture pattern (per repoWithBare in test/gate-hardening.test.mjs): one case with a working remote asserting the body edit, one with a broken remote asserting the ⚠ line and the skipped body edit

issue.body:
**From the harness review (finding 7, the last unswept silent failure).** `chalk evidence` attaches screenshots to the PR via blob-SHA URLs; the push path swallows errors (`catch {}`), so a failed push yields 404 image links in the PR body with no warning at the source.

**Why it wasn't fixed in the gate-hardening PR (#70):** pinning it honestly needs an e2e/gh worktree fixture (a bare-remote + stub-gh scaffold that exercises the real push path) — heavier than the one-line warn, and the wave's break-it rule forbids shipping the warn untested.

**Sketch:** reuse the `repoWithBare()` + stub-gh pattern from test/gate-hardening.test.mjs to drive `chalk evidence` against a push that fails, assert a ⚠ line and that the PR body skips (not 404s) the missing asset.

findings.note: Criterion 1 requires the ⚠ line to name the push failure AND its cause. The code prints `String(e.message || e).split('\n')[0]`, but for Node's execSync (lib/git.mjs git()) the error message is `Command failed: git push\n<stderr>` — the actual cause (git's stderr, e.g. 'does not appear to be a git repository', auth/network errors) is on subsequent lines and is discarded. The warning therefore only ever says 'Command failed: git push', which names the failure but not its cause. Use the later lines of e.message (or e.stderr) instead of line 0.

The locked test only asserts /⚠.*push failed/i for the failure case; it never asserts that the underlying cause (git stderr text) appears in the warning, so the cause-dropping bug above passes GREEN. A vacuous match on the fixed prefix cannot distinguish 'names the cause' from 'names only the command'.

On push failure the task still advances to stage 'tested' and t.evidence is recorded, and the emitted update says 'Evidence attached (N screenshot(s))' even though nothing was attached to the PR. The stage advance is locked by the test (re-run idempotency), but a later remote fix leaves the screenshots permanently unattached ('evidence (already done)'), and the update-feed title is misleading.

Happy path is structurally unchanged (same push→rev-parse→pr view→pr edit sequence gated behind pushed=true) and covered by the working-remote e2e case; no regression identified there. Note the locked test file itself does not appear in the diff/stat — it exists in the working tree but appears untracked; ensure it is committed with the change so the locked contract actually ships.

The prior block is resolved: runGit uses execSync with stdio 'pipe' + utf8, so e.stderr carries git's stderr; the cause extractor skips the 'Command failed: git push' line and the locked test now asserts the real git error text (/does not appear to be a git repository|Could not read from remote/), so the warning cannot regress to a vacuous prefix. Both criteria-4 cases drive the REAL push path (bare remote happy path asserting the pr-edit body with commit-SHA blob URLs; broken remote asserting the ⚠ cause line, the /NOT attached/ status line, the skipped pr edit via gh.log, and the local evidence commit). Reverting the fix would fail the failure-case test, so the coverage is non-vacuous.

test/evidence-push.test.mjs exists in the working tree and its sha256 is locked in .chalk/tasks.json, but it does not appear in the git diff/stat — it looks untracked. Ensure the locked test is committed alongside bin/chalk.mjs before merge, or the shipped change lands without its contract test in history.

On push failure the stage still advances to 'tested' and t.evidence is recorded, so a later remote fix leaves screenshots permanently unattached ('evidence (already done)'). This is now an explicit, locked design choice (the test asserts stage==='tested' for re-run idempotency) and the update-feed title honestly says 'push failed, not attached', so it is accepted — but a future 'evidence --retry-attach' path may be worth a follow-up task.

title: fix: sameModelFamily can't see env-var models (CHALK_OPENCODE_MODEL) — cross-model warning inert for opencode

acceptanceCriteria.text: sameModelFamily/modelSignature see the model of opencode-adapter commands whose model comes from the CHALK_OPENCODE_MODEL env var, so the doctor's same-model-reviewer warning can fire for opencode users
the fallback stays conservative: no env var and no --model flag means no model identity (no false alarms), and a non-opencode command is unaffected
a test covers: two opencode-adapter commands under the same CHALK_OPENCODE_MODEL → same family (warning fires); differing explicit --model still wins over the env var

issue.body:
**Known nit from the opencode integration (#65 review).** `modelSignature()` parses only the command string (binary + --model flag). opencode users set the model via `CHALK_OPENCODE_MODEL`, so the doctor's same-model-reviewer warning (self-preference bias) can never fire for them.

**Sketch:** teach `modelSignature` an env fallback per adapter (e.g. read `CHALK_OPENCODE_MODEL` when the command resolves to the opencode adapters), or let adapters declare their model via a comment/flag; keep it conservative (no false alarms).

findings.note: Adapter detection requires the script path at toks[1] (`node <path>`); a command like `node --experimental-x …/opencode-exec.mjs` or a quoted path with spaces escapes detection. Consistent with the documented adapter invocation and pre-existing tokenizer limits, but worth knowing.

The regex `/opencode-(exec|json)\.mjs$/` is only end-anchored, so a user script named e.g. `my-opencode-exec.mjs` would be misclassified as the bundled adapter and inherit the env-var model. Unlikely in practice; would only produce a warning, not a gate failure.

Two opencode adapters with NO env var and no --model still compare equal (bin 'opencode', model '' on both) and flag as same family. This mirrors the existing `claude -p` vs `claude -p` semantics (same binary + absent model is flagged) and is arguably correct — both would use opencode's default model — but no test pins sameModelFamily(EXEC, JSON_, {}) either way, so the intended semantics there are undocumented.

Coverage is genuinely adequate: reverting the lib/config.mjs change fails the locked unit tests (env-var resolution, --model precedence, non-opencode conservatism) AND the e2e doctor test proves the warning fires through the real process.env path via lib/doctor.mjs line 68. The env param is optional with a process.env default, so existing callers (doctor) and the prior locked crossmodel.test.mjs are unaffected — no regression found.

title: feat: chalk release --promote — protected-main release flow (promotion PR + tag on main's tip)

acceptanceCriteria.text: run the `--commit --no-tag` flow on the integration branch (github.base)
open the promotion PR to the deploy branch (new config, e.g. `github.deployBase` or `release.promoteTo`), wait for CI, merge with a MERGE commit regardless of github.mergeMethod
tag the deploy branch's tip vX.Y.Z and push the tag
collision/idempotency safety consistent with #93 and #91 (a failed step must not mark tasks released or strand a half-promoted release)

issue.body:
**Context:** the repo moved to a dev/main model (2026-07-06): all PRs target `dev` (protocol.github.base=dev), `main` is deployable and protected (PR required, `test` check, enforce_admins, no direct pushes). That protection rejects the push `chalk release --commit` (#93) makes to the branch it runs on.

**Working manual flow today:**
1. `chalk release --commit --no-tag` on **dev** (CHANGELOG + bump land as a normal commit)
2. promotion PR dev→main, merged with a **merge commit** (not squash — preserves the release commit SHA)
3. tag `vX.Y.Z` on main's tip and push the tag (tag pushes bypass branch protection; release.yml publishes the tagged tree as-is)

**Ask:** teach `chalk release` this path natively, e.g. `chalk release --promote`:
- [ ] run the `--commit --no-tag` flow on the integration branch (github.base)
- [ ] open the promotion PR to the deploy branch (new config, e.g. `github.deployBase` or `release.promoteTo`), wait for CI, merge with a MERGE commit regardless of github.mergeMethod
- [ ] tag the deploy branch's tip vX.Y.Z and push the tag
- [ ] collision/idempotency safety consistent with #93 and #91 (a failed step must not mark tasks released or strand a half-promoted release)

_filed while setting up the dev/main branch model_

findings.note: Criterion 4 is violated in the tag-created-but-push-failed window: if `git tag` (line 616) succeeds but `push origin vX` (line 621) fails, the die says 'Re-run to finish' — but the re-run CANNOT resume. Orphan detection (line 515) requires the tag to be ABSENT locally (`rev-parse refs/tags/vX === ''`), so with the local tag present resume=null, the collision probe is also skipped (wantTag=false under promote), and the run falls into the fresh path: it re-bumps from the already-bumped package.json (0.1.0→0.2.0), writes a second CHANGELOG section for the same tasks, stacks a second `chore(release)` commit, opens a second promotion PR, and never pushes v0.1.0 — the exact #91 version-skip bug reintroduced in the promote flow, plus a stranded half-promoted release. The 'idempotent tagging'/ls-remote skips at lines 615/619 are unreachable in the very scenario they were written for, because reaching them requires resume, and resume requires the tag to not exist.

Post-merge failures (fetch fails at line 613, or tag creation fails at 617) are also un-resumable against real GitHub: the re-run's resume path re-executes the FULL PR choreography — `pr list --state open` finds nothing (the PR is merged), so it runs `pr create dev→main`, which real `gh` rejects with 'no commits between main and dev' once the merge has landed, and the run dies at line 596 before ever reaching the tag step. The die messages promise 'The PR merged; re-run to finish the tagging', but the code never skips ahead to tagging when the deploy branch already contains the release commit. The locked test's gh stub unconditionally succeeds at `pr create`, masking this; the only re-run exercised (test 3) fails PRE-merge at RED CI.

#93's collision safety ('fail before anything is written') is dropped for --promote: because wantTag=false, the up-front probe at line 551 never runs. With a pre-existing stale local tag vX.Y.Z (pointing anywhere), promote writes the release commit, opens/merges the PR (moving protected main), then line 615 silently SKIPS tag creation because the tag exists, and line 619 pushes the stale tag — publishing a vX.Y.Z tag pointing at the wrong commit while marking all tasks released. No collision case is tested for promote.

The idempotency machinery specific to promote is untested: no test injects a failure after `pr merge` (tag-push failure, fetch failure), so the tag-exists skip (615), the ls-remote-guarded push (619), and the merged-fallback `pr view` recheck (607-608) can all be deleted or inverted with the suite staying green. Test 3's re-run only covers the pre-merge RED-CI interruption, which exercises the pre-existing #91 orphan detection, not the promote-specific recovery the criterion demands. The 'CI still pending' abort path is likewise unpinned (only bucket:fail is tested).

The stub's `pr merge` performs a fast-forward `push dev:main`, so the deploy tip SHA equals dev's HEAD — the tests cannot distinguish 'tag the deploy tip' from 'tag local HEAD'. A real --merge produces a merge commit whose SHA differs from dev HEAD; the implementation does tag FETCH_HEAD correctly, but the locked assertion (remoteMain === dev HEAD === tag target) would also pass an implementation that tags HEAD, weakening criterion 3's pin.

The diff again bundles unrelated spine changes (new `protocol.worktree` config block in .chalk/chalk.json, imported tasks #92 and the already-done #91 task/board entries) against the repo's 'keep diffs small and scoped' rule — carried-over noise, not a gate by itself.

The find-or-create PR resolution (line 612 `pr list --head … --state open`) is exercised by test 3's re-run but never ASSERTED: test 3 doesn't count `pr create` lines, and the stub's `pr create` happily succeeds when a PR already exists, so deleting the `pr list` lookup keeps the suite green while real gh would die with 'a pull request already exists' on every pre-merge re-run — an un-resumable pre-merge interruption in production. Add `creates.length === 1` to test 3 (mirroring test 4) next cycle.

The later-arrival cutoff on promote RESUME (lines 644-645: tasks done after the orphan commit are deferred to the next release) is untested in the promote suite — mutating `cutoff` to Infinity on resume stays green, silently absorbing post-interruption tasks into a frozen CHANGELOG. The equivalent behavior is pinned only in release-recovery's non-promote path; the promote path shares the shape but not a test.

The completion marker remains the working-tree `Released vX` decision: on a fresh clone missing .chalk/decisions.md, the first promote after a completed release false-resumes on the old release commit — but the cutoff excludes newer tasks and origin already has the tag, so it degrades to a confusing no-op 'released vOLD' success plus a duplicate decision, then self-heals on the next run. Same accepted residual as the #91 pass review; decisions.md is committed, so the window is narrow.

The `pr view --json state` merged-fallback (lines 628-630) and the ls-remote tag-already-pushed skip (line 638) are individually revertible with the suite green (the stub's merge never fails non-zero-but-merged, and re-pushing an identical tag is a no-op). Both are defensive mirrors of existing `chalk merge` behavior rather than criterion-bearing paths.

All four previously blocking findings are demonstrably fixed and pinned by the amended locked test: the post-merge tag-push failure now resumes (decision-keyed detection tolerates the leftover local tag, `tag -fa` + ls-remote guard, test 4 asserts ONE `pr create` and 'already merged'), the merged-PR re-run skips the choreography via fetch-first `merge-base --is-ancestor`, the fresh-path collision probe (local+remote, line 561) fails before any write (test 5), pending-CI exhaustion aborts (test 6), and the stub's real commit-tree merge makes 'tag the deploy TIP' distinguishable from 'tag local HEAD' (test 1 asserts remoteMain ≠ dev HEAD and tag→merge-commit). Criterion 2's 'regardless of mergeMethod' is honestly pinned: the config carries the default squash while the log asserts `pr merge 7 --merge`. The lingering diff noise (worktree config block, imported task entries) is carried-over spine state, not code risk.

reviews.note: locked test amended after review — re-review required

findings.note: The remote-tag arm of the fresh-path collision probe (line 561, `ls-remote --tags origin`) is revertible with the suite green: test 5 only plants a LOCAL stale tag. If origin carries vX.Y.Z but the local clone doesn't (fresh clone after a foreign push), deleting that arm lets promote commit + merge, then line 638's ls-remote skip silently treats the stale remote tag as 'already shipped' and marks everything released against a wrong-pointing tag. Add a remote-only-collision case next cycle.

`push origin ${ghBase}` (line 609) is revertible with the suite green: the gh stub's `pr merge` builds the merge commit from the LOCAL dev ref and pushes the objects itself, so the deploy-tip/tag/package.json assertions all pass even if the release commit never reaches origin/dev — against real GitHub the promotion PR would not contain the release commit. An assertion that the bare repo's dev ref equals the local release commit would pin criterion 1's push.

Two defensive paths remain individually revertible: the `pr view --json state` merged-despite-nonzero fallback (lines 628-630; the stub's merge never fails-but-merges) and the ls-remote tag-already-pushed skip (line 638; re-pushing an identical tag is a no-op). Both mirror existing `chalk merge` behavior and are not criterion-bearing — same accepted residual as the prior review.

The completion marker is still the working-tree `Released vX` decision: on a clone missing .chalk/decisions.md the first promote after a completed release false-resumes on the old release commit, degrading to a confusing no-op 'released vOLD' plus a duplicate decision before self-healing. Same narrow accepted residual as #91; decisions.md is committed.

The diff still bundles unrelated spine noise (the `protocol.worktree` config block in .chalk/chalk.json, imported #92/#91 task and board entries) against the 'keep diffs small and scoped' rule — carried-over state, not code risk.

Non-promote paths verified unchanged: `wantTag` only adds `!promote`, and the #91 resume gate `(promote || !tagLocal)` preserves the tag-absent requirement for --commit; release-recovery's locked contract is untouched. Both prior blocking reviews' findings are demonstrably fixed and now pinned by the amended locked test: post-merge tag-push failure resumes past the leftover local tag (test 4, ONE `pr create`, 'already merged'), the merged-PR re-run skips choreography via fetch-first `merge-base --is-ancestor`, the fresh collision probe fails before any write (test 5), pending-CI exhaustion aborts (test 6), the pre-merge re-run FINDS the open PR (test 3 `creates.length===1`), late arrivals are deferred on resume (test 3), and the stub's real commit-tree merge makes 'tag the deploy TIP' distinguishable from 'tag local HEAD' (test 1).

title: chore: author the held-out regression set — audit warns 0 tests vs a floor of 4 (~10k LOC)

acceptanceCriteria.text: regression.command and regression.authorCommand are configured (node --test .chalk/held-out + a BYO claude author); .chalk/held-out stays gitignored
chalk guard gen authors at least 4 held-out test files derived from the spec (the size floor for ~10k LOC), locked into regression.tests
chalk audit runs GREEN with the understaffing warning gone
implementer blindness holds: the tests are written by the BYO guard author subprocess; this session never reads .chalk/held-out contents

issue.body:
`chalk audit` is GREEN but warns:

> ⚠ held-out set (0) is below the size floor (4 for 9919 LOC) — P7 stringency scales with code; author more via `chalk guard`.

Dogfooding gap: the protocol's own P7 lever is unarmed. Author 4+ held-out tests from the spec's durable criteria (`chalk guard gen` can draft them), keep `.chalk/held-out/` gitignored per the doctor rule.

_filed during the 2026-07-06 issue sweep_

block.reason: The held-out set must be authored OUTSIDE this session (implementer blindness): the sandbox classifier refuses to let this agent spawn an auto-writing author into .chalk/held-out. Human steps: run 'chalk guard gen' in your own terminal (authorCommand is configured), then set protocol.regression.command='node --test .chalk/held-out', run 'chalk audit' (expect GREEN, floor 5 for ~10k LOC), and 'chalk unblock task-2cae7c80'.

title: fix: release --commit partial-failure recovery — a post-commit tag failure leaves an untagged release commit, and a re-run version-skips

acceptanceCriteria.text: On a post-commit tag failure, either roll the release commit back (`git reset --hard HEAD~1` is safe — the commit contains only the release artifacts) or make the re-run detect the orphaned release commit and tag it instead of re-bumping
Test the recovery path (tag fails once after the commit → next run converges on ONE release commit + tag)
While there: cover `--commit --no-tag` (commits the bump, skips the probe) with a test

issue.body:
From the task-021f498 adversarial review (low, non-blocking, shipped as-is).

`chalk release --commit` probes the tag collision up front, but if `git tag` fails AFTER the release commit for a non-collision reason (hook, perms, probe→tag race), `die()` leaves an untagged `chore(release)` commit at HEAD with the bumped package.json. A re-run then bumps FROM the already-bumped version (version skip) and produces a second release commit.

- [ ] On a post-commit tag failure, either roll the release commit back (`git reset --hard HEAD~1` is safe — the commit contains only the release artifacts) or make the re-run detect the orphaned release commit and tag it instead of re-bumping
- [ ] Test the recovery path (tag fails once after the commit → next run converges on ONE release commit + tag)
- [ ] While there: cover `--commit --no-tag` (commits the bump, skips the probe) with a test

_filed from the chalk review of task-021f498_

findings.note: The resume detector cannot distinguish an INTERRUPTED --commit run from a completed, INTENTIONAL `--commit --no-tag` release — which is now this repo's own release path (dev/main promote flow, #98/decision evt-0e49e19e: `release --commit --no-tag` on dev, tag lands on main's tip). After such a release, HEAD on dev is a `chore(release): vX` commit whose tag is legitimately absent locally (never created there, or on main and not yet fetched). The next `release --commit` with new done tasks then falsely 'resumes': it tags vOLD onto dev's commit and/or marks the NEW tasks released at the OLD version with no changelog entry and no bump — new work is silently swallowed. No test covers 'completed --no-tag release, then a subsequent release'; test 2 only covers the tagged-success case.

On resume, `for (const t of tasks) t.released = v` marks ALL currently releasable tasks released at the orphan version — including tasks that became done AFTER the interrupted run. Those tasks are absorbed into a v that already has a frozen CHANGELOG entry not mentioning them, so they never appear in any release notes. The interrupted run's task set should be what gets marked (or later arrivals excluded); untested.

Orphan detection inspects only HEAD's subject (`log -1`). If any commit lands on top of the orphaned release commit before the re-run — routine on a shared dev branch or under `chalk run` — the recovery is skipped and the original bug (bump-from-bumped version skip + stacked release commit) returns. Criterion 1 is satisfied only for an immediate re-run with an untouched HEAD; this limitation is neither handled nor pinned by a test. Also `--dry-run` returns before the recovery block, so a post-interruption dry-run previews the wrong, double-bumped version.

Test 1 is genuinely non-vacuous (the refs/tags/v0.1.0/block injection is a real post-commit tag failure, and the convergence assertions fail if the recovery is reverted), and test 2 guards false-resume after a tagged success — good. But test 3 (--no-tag) stops at the first release; nothing asserts that an intentional --no-tag release commit at HEAD doesn't poison a later run, which is exactly the high-severity gap above.

The diff bundles unrelated spine changes — a new `protocol.worktree` block in .chalk/chalk.json and two unrelated imported tasks (#98, #92) — against the repo rule 'keep diffs small and scoped to the current task'. Not blocking on its own, but it widens the review surface of a release-safety fix.

The completion marker (`Released vX` in .chalk/decisions.md) is written to the WORKING TREE only — the release commit itself contains just CHANGELOG.md + package.json. After an intentional `--commit --no-tag` release, if the decisions.md change is lost before the next run (fresh clone, new worktree, checkout/reset, or the spine push not yet landed), the completed release commit again looks like an orphan and the false-resume the prior review blocked (tag vOLD, swallow new work) returns. The locked test only exercises the same-working-tree case; in-repo flows push the spine so this is an edge, but it is the one hole left in the discriminator.

The new dry-run resume branch (line 509: post-interruption `--dry-run` previews 'would RESUME' instead of a double-bumped version — explicitly called out in the prior review) has no test; reverting just that line leaves the suite green. Also nothing pins the resume-fails-again path (tag fails a second time → 'Nothing was marked released').

Orphan detection scans only `log -50`; an orphan buried >50 commits deep (plausible under `chalk run` on a busy dev branch) silently reverts to the original bump-from-bumped behavior. Likewise a plain `release` (no --commit) run after an interruption bypasses the recovery block entirely and tags HEAD at a skipped version — outside the stated criteria but an adjacent trap. If the orphan's %cI can't be parsed, cutoff=0 defers ALL tasks and tags an empty release.

The completion check is a substring regex over all of decisions.md — any decision title/why that happens to contain `Released vX.Y.Z` for the orphan's version (e.g. a forced/manual note about that version) marks it completed and suppresses a legitimate resume. Narrow, but the marker is not namespaced.

The diff again bundles unrelated spine changes (new `protocol.worktree` config block, imported tasks #98/#92, board entries) against the 'keep diffs small and scoped' rule. The locked-test amendment itself went through the sanctioned path (amend-spec decision logged, sha256 updated), and all four previously blocking findings are now each pinned by a dedicated locked test (decision marker, --no-tag non-orphan, buried orphan, late-arrival deferral), so this is noise, not a gate.

reviews.note: locked test amended after review — re-review required

findings.note: Criterion 3's parenthetical 'skips the probe' is not directly pinned: test 3 ('--commit --no-tag') runs in a repo with no colliding tag, so it would still pass if the up-front collision probe ran under --no-tag. The observable of probe-skipping (a pre-existing v0.1.0 tag does not abort a --no-tag release) is unasserted. Non-blocking because the probe-guard (`wantTag && withCommit` at line 539) is pre-existing behavior, not part of this change, and the criterion's main clause — cover the --commit --no-tag flow — is met (commit exists, no tag, released marked, bump landed).

The completion marker ('Released vX' in .chalk/decisions.md) still lives outside the release commit (which contains only CHANGELOG.md + package.json). If the decisions.md append is lost before the next run (fresh clone/worktree, reset, spine push not landed), a completed --no-tag release commit re-qualifies as an orphan and the false-resume returns. The locked tests only exercise the same-working-tree case. In-repo flows commit/push the spine, so this is an accepted edge carried over from the prior pass review, not a new defect.

The resume-fails-again path (tag fails a SECOND time during resume → die 'Nothing was marked released', line 512) is untested; deleting that catch leaves the suite green (crash vs. clean die is indistinguishable to the tests). The dry-run resume preview flagged last round IS now pinned and non-vacuous (test 5 lines 112–116: reverting line 509 yields 'release v0.2.0 (dry-run)', which fails both /would RESUME/ and doesNotMatch(/v0\.2\.0/)).

Residual edges, all outside the stated criteria: orphan scan depth is `log -50` (an orphan buried deeper silently reverts to bump-from-bumped); the completion check is an un-namespaced substring regex over all of decisions.md (a manual note containing 'Released vX.Y.Z' suppresses a legitimate resume); an unparseable %cI yields cutoff=0, deferring ALL tasks into an empty resumed release; a plain `release` (no --commit) after an interruption bypasses the recovery block entirely.

The diff bundles unrelated spine changes (new `protocol.worktree` config block, imported tasks #98/#92, board entries) against the 'keep diffs small and scoped' rule — noise, not a gate. The amend-spec path was used correctly (two amendment decisions logged, sha256 updated in tasks.json), and each previously blocking finding — decision-marker discriminator, intentional --no-tag non-orphan, buried orphan, late-arrival deferral, dry-run resume preview — is now pinned by a dedicated locked test that fails if the corresponding behavior is reverted. All three acceptance criteria are substantively satisfied.
